title: Kubernetes
objectives: Understand Kubernetes via a real-life analogy
Know the core objects: Pod, Deployment, Service, Ingress, ConfigMap, Secret
Write a real-world Deployment + Service manifest

sections.markdown:
**Think of Kubernetes as an air traffic control system for containers.**

- A busy airport has HUNDREDS of planes (containers). Each plane needs a gate (host), fuel (memory), a runway (CPU), a flight plan (config). Passengers need to reach their planes reliably (networking). When one plane breaks down, another takes off in its place (self-healing). During peak hours, more planes are dispatched (autoscaling). When a runway closes, planes reroute (failover).
- **Air traffic control** doesn't fly the planes — it TELLS airport ground staff (kubelet on each node) which plane goes where, when to refuel, when to retire an aging plane, when to spin up more. Controllers watch the state; when reality drifts from the plan, they nudge it back.

**In code:** Kubernetes is a container orchestrator. You DECLARE the desired state ('I want 3 replicas of my app, each with 512MB RAM, exposed on port 8080') as YAML manifests; Kubernetes CONTROLLERS continuously reconcile actual state to match. Pod dies? Scheduler spins another. Node dies? All its pods reschedule elsewhere. Traffic spikes? Horizontal Pod Autoscaler adds replicas.

**Concretely:** you no longer say 'ssh to server-42, deploy the new jar, restart tomcat'. You say `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml` and K8s figures out where to run the new pods, how to gracefully drain the old ones, how to update DNS, how to keep the service reachable throughout. **This is the standard deployment model for backend systems since ~2018.**

**Fair warning:** Kubernetes is HUGE. There are 40+ core resource types, YAML is verbose, the learning curve is real. Learn Pod / Deployment / Service / Ingress / ConfigMap / Secret / Probes deeply — that's 80% of daily use. Skip StatefulSets, DaemonSets, CRDs until you need them.

**Kubernetes** (K8s) is a container orchestration platform originally from Google (2014, open-sourced), based on Google's internal Borg system. Manages CONTAINERIZED workloads across a CLUSTER of machines. Core primitives: **Pod** (smallest deployable unit — one or more tightly-coupled containers), **Deployment** (declarative pod management with rolling updates), **Service** (stable network endpoint for pods), **Ingress** (external HTTP routing), **ConfigMap** / **Secret** (config injection). Fully DECLARATIVE — you describe the desired state; controllers reconcile actual state continuously. Standard for backend deployment since ~2018.
## The core objects you'll use every day

rows: One or more containers running together; ephemeral
Manages ReplicaSets of pods; rolling updates + rollback
Stable virtual IP + DNS in front of pods (load balances)
Non-secret config as key/value or files
Sensitive config (base64-encoded, ideally encrypted at rest)
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler; scales replicas by CPU/mem/custom metrics

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — production-grade Deployment + Service + Ingress

sections.code:
# ============ Deployment ============
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: orders-api
  namespace: prod
  labels: { app: orders-api }
spec:
  replicas: 3                         # 3 pods in parallel
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0                # zero downtime
      maxSurge: 1                       # 1 extra pod during rollout
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: orders-api }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: orders-api }
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: orders-api
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: registry.io/myorg/orders-api:1.2.3   # PIN version, never :latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
          value: prod
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef: { name: orders-db, key: password }
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef: { name: orders-config }
        resources:                     # REQUIRED — scheduler + eviction depend on this
          requests: { cpu: 200m, memory: 512Mi }
          limits:   { cpu: 1000m, memory: 1Gi }
        # Three probes — each has a distinct job
        startupProbe:                  # slow starters (Java) — grace period on boot
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health, port: 8080 }
          initialDelaySeconds: 20
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 30         # up to 150s to start
        readinessProbe:                # in the load-balancer? — remove when Redis/DB is down
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:                 # still alive? — kill + restart if deadlocked
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/liveness, port: 8080 }
          initialDelaySeconds: 60
          periodSeconds: 15
---
# ============ Service ============
# Stable virtual IP + DNS name. Pods come and go; the Service stays.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: orders-api
  namespace: prod
spec:
  selector: { app: orders-api }
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
  type: ClusterIP                       # internal only; use Ingress for external
---
# ============ Ingress ============
# External HTTP → routes to Service based on host + path.
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: orders-api
  namespace: prod
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod   # auto TLS
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts: [api.example.com]
    secretName: api-tls
  rules:
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /orders
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: orders-api
            port: { number: 80 }

# Apply:
#   kubectl apply -f orders-api.yaml
#   kubectl rollout status deployment/orders-api
#   kubectl get pods,svc,ing -n prod

sections.markdown: ## Probes — the three that matter

rows: During startup: give more grace. After success, liveness takes over.
Is the app currently ABLE to serve traffic?
REMOVE from Service load balancer (traffic paused). Pod not killed.
Is the app ALIVE or deadlocked?

sections.markdown: **The distinction that trips juniors:** if a pod's Redis dependency drops for 20 seconds, readiness fails → K8s stops sending traffic → GOOD (users get a healthier peer). Liveness should NOT fail here — killing the pod won't fix Redis. Set liveness to check the pod's own thread/state, not external deps. Getting these wrong causes 'my pods keep restarting whenever Redis blips' cascades.
## Rolling updates + rollback

sections.code:
# Update the image; K8s rolls new pods in gradually (maxSurge, maxUnavailable).
kubectl set image deployment/orders-api app=registry.io/myorg/orders-api:1.3.0

# Watch the rollout live.
kubectl rollout status deployment/orders-api

# Something broke? Roll back to the previous version.
kubectl rollout undo deployment/orders-api

# See history.
kubectl rollout history deployment/orders-api

# Pause the rollout (for canary-style manual gate).
kubectl rollout pause deployment/orders-api
# Resume later.
kubectl rollout resume deployment/orders-api

# Combined with:
#   - readiness probes → K8s doesn't send traffic to a NEW pod until it's ready
#   - maxUnavailable: 0 → NEVER drop below N healthy pods during rollout
#   - maxSurge: 1 → briefly run N+1 pods during rollout
# You get zero-downtime deploys automatically. This is K8s's superpower.

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: No resource `requests` or `limits`.** Scheduler doesn't know how to place your pods; noisy-neighbor pods OOM others. Always set both.
**Mistake 2: `image: latest`.** Which version is running RIGHT NOW? Impossible to know. Pin explicit tags: `1.2.3` or a SHA digest.
**Mistake 3: Missing readiness probe.** K8s sends traffic to pods before they've finished starting → 500s during rollouts.
**Mistake 4: Same endpoint for liveness + readiness.** A Redis blip fails both → K8s restarts the pod. Should have paused traffic only.
**Mistake 5: Secrets in ConfigMaps or env-inline.** Use `Secret` resources; enable encryption at rest; consider ExternalSecrets Operator with Vault.
**Mistake 6: `kubectl apply` from local kubeconfig to production.** No audit trail, no code review, no rollback plan. Use GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux).
**Mistake 7: Debugging with `kubectl exec` in prod.** No logs of what changed. Use `kubectl logs`, distributed tracing, or ephemeral debug containers (`kubectl debug`).
## Best practices
**1. `requests` + `limits` on every container.** Scheduler + eviction depend on this.
**2. Explicit image tags.** `:1.2.3` or `@sha256:...`, never `:latest`.
**3. Distinct startup + readiness + liveness probes.** Each has a job.
**4. Rolling update with maxUnavailable: 0.** Zero-downtime by default.
**5. Secrets — real Secrets resource + encryption at rest + Vault integration.**
**6. Namespaces per environment.** `dev`, `staging`, `prod`. Isolation + RBAC.
**7. GitOps for deployments.** ArgoCD or Flux reconciling from git. Auditable, revertable.
**8. NetworkPolicies for service-to-service.** Default-deny; whitelist expected paths.
**9. Horizontal Pod Autoscaler on production Deployments.** Handle traffic spikes automatically.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Local cluster.** Install `minikube` or `kind`; deploy an nginx Deployment + Service; port-forward to localhost.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Real app.** Deploy your Spring Boot Dockerfile; add ConfigMap for env, Secret for DB password.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Rolling update.** Change image; watch `kubectl rollout status`. Break it deliberately; `kubectl rollout undo`.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Probes.** Add distinct startup / readiness / liveness endpoints; simulate DB down; verify readiness fails but liveness doesn't.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Ingress + TLS.** Install ingress-nginx + cert-manager; automatic Let's Encrypt cert on `api.example.com`.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — GitOps.** Install ArgoCD; sync a Deployment from git; break the git state; watch ArgoCD reconcile.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Kubernetes and what problem does it solve?**

**A:** Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform — it manages CONTAINERIZED workloads across a CLUSTER of machines. You declare the DESIRED state (via YAML manifests: 'I want 3 replicas of my app, each with 512MB RAM, exposed on port 8080'); K8s CONTROLLERS continuously reconcile ACTUAL state to match. **Problems it solves:** scheduling containers across hosts, service discovery (pods come and go; DNS stays), load balancing, rolling updates with zero downtime, self-healing (dead pod → new pod), autoscaling, secret and config injection, storage lifecycle. **Before K8s:** Chef/Puppet + custom scripts + Nagios + bash duct tape. **With K8s:** one declarative model for the entire stack. Standard for backend deployment since ~2018.

**Q2: What is a Pod and how is it different from a container?**

**A:** A **Pod** is the smallest deployable unit in K8s. It's ONE OR MORE CONTAINERS that share the same network namespace (same IP + port space) and can share volumes. Most pods have ONE container; multi-container pods use sidecars for cross-cutting concerns (logging, service mesh proxy, config reloader). **Pods are EPHEMERAL** — they can die, get killed, get rescheduled. Their IP changes. Never reference a pod IP directly. Instead: use a **Service**, which provides a stable virtual IP + DNS name that load-balances across pods matching its selector. **Rule of thumb:** app + logging sidecar + service-mesh proxy = same pod (share networking). Different services = different pods (independent lifecycle).

**Q3: What are the differences between Startup, Readiness, and Liveness probes?**

**A:** **Startup** — has the app finished BOOTING? Runs first; disables liveness/readiness during boot. Perfect for slow starters (JVMs). Failure → still starting. **Readiness** — can this pod SERVE TRAFFIC right now? Fails when a critical dependency (Redis, DB) is temporarily down. K8s REMOVES the pod from the Service's load balancer (traffic redirects to healthy peers); pod NOT killed. Fast recovery when the dependency returns. **Liveness** — is this pod ALIVE or DEADLOCKED? Fails when the process is stuck in some way a restart would fix. K8s KILLS the container and starts a new one. **Common mistake:** using the same endpoint for both — a Redis blip fails both → K8s restarts pods → cascade of restarts across all pods. Distinct endpoints; distinct meanings; distinct failure actions.

**Q4: What is a Service and what types are there?**

**A:** A **Service** is a stable virtual IP + DNS name in front of a set of pods (matched by label selector). Load-balances requests across pod endpoints. **Types:** **(1) ClusterIP** (default) — internal-only IP, reachable within the cluster. Use for service-to-service. **(2) NodePort** — exposes on every node's IP at a static port (30000-32767). Cheap external access, ugly URLs. **(3) LoadBalancer** — provisions a cloud LB (AWS ELB, GCP LB); external IP. Real production external exposure. **(4) ExternalName** — DNS CNAME to an external service (proxy). **(5) Headless (`clusterIP: None`)** — DNS returns pod IPs directly; used with StatefulSets. **Rule:** ClusterIP for internal; LoadBalancer + Ingress for external HTTP. Never expose apps via NodePort in production.

**Q5: What is an Ingress?**

**A:** An Ingress is a K8s resource that routes EXTERNAL HTTP/S traffic to Services based on hostname + path. It's a smart HTTP-aware load balancer above your Services. **Example:** `api.example.com/orders` → `orders-api` Service; `api.example.com/users` → `users-api` Service. One external IP + certificate handles many services. **How it works:** an **Ingress Controller** (typically nginx-ingress, Traefik, or a cloud provider one) watches Ingress resources and configures itself. **Bonus features:** TLS termination (via `cert-manager` for automatic Let's Encrypt), rate limiting, path rewriting, sticky sessions. **Contrast with LoadBalancer Service:** LoadBalancer gives one external IP per service (expensive at scale); Ingress lets many services share one IP + cert. **Rule:** for HTTP APIs, Ingress. For non-HTTP (databases, gRPC without HTTP/2 proxy support), LoadBalancer.

**Q6: What is a Deployment vs a StatefulSet?**

**A:** **Deployment** — for STATELESS apps. Pods are INTERCHANGEABLE (any pod can serve any request). Managed via ReplicaSets. Rolling updates, easy scaling, no persistent identity. Use for: web servers, APIs, most microservices. **StatefulSet** — for STATEFUL apps. Pods have STABLE, UNIQUE identities (`app-0`, `app-1`, `app-2`) and STABLE STORAGE (each pod has its own PersistentVolumeClaim). Ordered deployment, ordered scaling. Use for: databases (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB), distributed systems (Kafka, Zookeeper). **Key differences:** Deployment pods are cattle (identical); StatefulSet pods are pets (each has an identity). Deployment updates are parallel; StatefulSet updates are ordered (0 → 1 → 2, waiting for each to be ready). **Rule:** if you're unsure, use Deployment. StatefulSets are for specific data-storage workloads.

**Q7: How do rolling updates work in Kubernetes?**

**A:** A Deployment update (change image, config, resources, etc.) triggers a rolling update. **Mechanism:** K8s creates a NEW ReplicaSet with the new pod template; scales it up while scaling the OLD ReplicaSet down. Speed governed by `maxSurge` (how many EXTRA pods allowed during rollout — e.g., 1 or 25%) and `maxUnavailable` (how many pods can be DOWN — 0 for zero-downtime, 1 for faster). Readiness probes ensure NEW pods aren't sent traffic until healthy. Old pods drain gracefully via SIGTERM → readiness fails → traffic redirects → `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` elapses → force-kill. **Rollback:** `kubectl rollout undo deployment/x` — K8s reverses the ReplicaSets. **Rollout history** kept by default. **This is Kubernetes' single biggest ops win** — safe deploys without custom orchestration.

**Q8: How do you handle secrets in Kubernetes?**

**A:** K8s has a **Secret** resource — base64-encoded key/value pairs, mounted as env vars or files. **PROBLEMS:** base64 is NOT encryption; secrets are stored in etcd in plaintext by default; RBAC controls access but a pod that reads a secret sees it in clear. **Best practices:** **(1) Enable encryption at rest** for etcd. **(2) Restrict Secret RBAC** — only pods that need a secret can read it. **(3) Use ExternalSecrets Operator with HashiCorp Vault / AWS Secrets Manager** — the K8s Secret is a copy synced from the source of truth, rotated automatically. **(4) SealedSecrets or SOPS** — commit ENCRYPTED secret manifests to git; K8s controller decrypts on apply. **(5) Never** commit unencrypted Secret manifests. **Never** put secrets in ConfigMaps (no RBAC around ConfigMaps). **Never** hard-code them in images.

## Real-world usage

- **Every non-trivial backend deploy** since ~2018 targets Kubernetes.
- **Managed K8s** — EKS (AWS), GKE (Google), AKS (Azure), DOKS. Local dev: minikube, kind, k3d.
- **Ecosystem** — Helm (package manager), ArgoCD/Flux (GitOps), cert-manager (TLS), Prometheus (metrics), Grafana (dashboards), Istio/Linkerd (service mesh).
- **CNCF projects** — Kubernetes is graduate; hundreds of ecosystem projects.
- **Alternatives** — Nomad, ECS (AWS-only), Cloud Run / App Engine (serverless containers) for smaller-scale simplicity.

## Advanced notes

- **Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)** — extend K8s with your own resource types (Argo Rollouts, Cert-Manager, ExternalDNS).
- **Operators** — controllers that manage complex apps (Postgres Operator, Kafka Operator) declaratively.
- **Helm** — package manager; templated YAML.
- **Kustomize** — patch-based YAML customization; built into `kubectl`.
- **HorizontalPodAutoscaler** — scale replicas based on metrics.
- **VerticalPodAutoscaler** — adjust requests/limits automatically.
- **Cluster Autoscaler** — add/remove nodes based on pod pressure.
- **PodDisruptionBudget** — protect against too many pods being killed simultaneously (during node drains).
- **NetworkPolicies** — service-to-service firewall rules.
- **Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd)** — L7 traffic control + mTLS + observability without code changes.
- **Multi-tenant** — namespaces + RBAC + resource quotas + network policies.
- **KubeVirt** — run VMs inside K8s.
- **Karpenter** (AWS) — smarter cluster autoscaler.
- **`k9s`** — TUI for cluster inspection.
- **`stern`** — multi-pod log tailer.

flashcards.back: Container orchestrator: you declare desired state; controllers reconcile continuously.
Smallest unit; one+ containers sharing network + volumes. Ephemeral.
Declarative pod management; rolling updates + rollback for stateless apps.
Stable virtual IP + DNS in front of pods; load balances.
External HTTP router with host/path rules + TLS termination.
Startup (finished booting?), Readiness (able to serve?), Liveness (alive or deadlocked?).

challenges.text: Install kind; deploy nginx Deployment + Service; port-forward.
kubectl apply Spring Boot Deployment + ConfigMap + Secret.
Rolling update via image change; then rollback.
Add all three probes; simulate DB down; verify only readiness fails.
Ingress + cert-manager auto TLS on real domain.
HPA scaling on CPU; drive load with hey/ab; watch replicas grow.
GitOps with ArgoCD: sync from git; break git state; watch reconciliation.

interviewQuestions: What is Kubernetes and what problem does it solve?
What is a Pod and how is it different from a container?
What are the differences between Startup, Readiness, and Liveness probes?
What is a Service and what types are there?
What is a Deployment vs a StatefulSet?
How do rolling updates work in Kubernetes?
How do you handle secrets in Kubernetes?
